title: Data
text:
The primary data source is the Burundi Priority Household Panel Survey (Enquête Prioritaire des Ménages), a three-wave longitudinal household survey covering rural Burundi.






Note



Full citation: Burundi Priority Household Panel Survey 1998–2007–2012, European Centre for Advanced Research in Economics and Statistics (ECARES), Université libre de Bruxelles. Data collected in collaboration with the World Bank and ISTEEBU (Institut de Statistiques et d’Études Économiques du Burundi).


Wave 1 — Baseline (1998): The first wave was conducted in 1998 by the World Bank and ISTEEBU. It covered 3,900 rural households in 390 rural clusters (sous-collines) plus 2,400 urban households, providing a nationally representative sample of rural Burundi. The survey collected detailed information on household composition, residence, health, education, economic activities and welfare, and child anthropometrics.
Wave 2 — First Follow-up (2007): The second wave, conducted in 2007, re-visited 100 randomly selected rural clusters from the original 390. Researchers successfully re-interviewed 872 original (parental) households and 536 split-off households (new households formed between waves by members of original households), for a total of 1,408 households. The 2007 follow-up also collected retrospective annual data on each household for the entire 1998–2007 period, including migration events, conflict exposure, and shock experiences. This retrospective structure is central to our identification strategy.
Wave 3 — Second Follow-up (2012): A third wave in 2012 re-interviewed 1,239 households, including a new module on intra-household decision-making administered to both spouses. This paper uses Waves 1 and 2 only.




Table 1: Burundi Household Panel Survey <U+2014> Wave Summary






Wave
Year
Type
Rural HHs
Coverage
Key Modules




Wave 1
1998
Baseline
3,900
390 clusters (nationally representative)
HH composition, health, education, welfare, anthropometrics


Wave 2
2007
Follow-up
872 original + 536 split-off
100 randomly selected clusters
All Wave 1 modules + retrospective annual migration/shocks 1998<U+2013>2007


Wave 3
2012
Follow-up
1,239
Same 100 clusters
All prior modules + intra-household decision-making











The transition from Wave 1 (3,900 HHs) to Wave 2 (872 original HHs in the analytical sample) reflects two stages: (1) the sampling of 100 clusters from 390, and (2) within-cluster attrition over 1998–2007.
Among the 1,000 households in the 100 selected clusters, 87.4% were successfully re-interviewed (874 households), with attrition broken down as follows:




Table 2: Wave 1 <U+2192> Wave 2 Attrition Analysis






Category
Share (%)
Notes




Successfully re-interviewed
87.4%
872 original + 536 split-off households


Deceased head of household
2.8%
Natural or conflict-related death


Moved <U+2014> location unknown
2.5%
Possible conflict displacement


Disappeared
2.4%
Could not locate family


Household dissolved
3.6%
Merge into other household


Unknown
1.3%
No information available



Notes:  Source: Verwimp & Bundervoet (2009). Attrition analysis shows no systematic selection on poverty or violence exposure.











Critically, Verwimp & Bundervoet (2009) demonstrate that attrition is not systematically related to baseline poverty or conflict exposure — the two main variables of interest in our analysis. This mitigates concerns about endogenous sample selection.

Our analytical sample applies the following restrictions to the Wave 2 dataset:

title: Data
text:
Conflict Data

The Burundian Civil War (1993–2005)
The Burundian civil war began on October 21, 1993, when President Melchior Ndadaye — Burundi’s first democratically elected president and first Hutu head of state — was assassinated in a coup attempt by Tutsi soldiers of the national army. His assassination triggered widespread ethnic violence across rural Burundi, pitting Hutu rebel groups against the Tutsi-led military government.






Important



Scale of the conflict: The war resulted in an estimated 300,000 deaths over the 1993–2005 period (and some estimates of 50,000–100,000 deaths in the first year alone), displaced over 1.2 million people, and caused Burundi’s GDP to contract by approximately 50% over the war period. Agriculture — employing over 90% of the rural population and contributing more than 40% to GDP — suffered severely from forced displacement, crop destruction, livestock theft, and land seizure.


Key milestones of the conflict:




Table 4: Civil War Timeline <U+2014> Key Events






Date
Event




October 21, 1993
Assassination of President Melchior Ndadaye <U+2192> conflict erupts


1994<U+2013>1997
Escalation: CNDD-FDD, FNL, and other groups fight against Tutsi-led government


June 1998
**Wave 1 survey**: HPS baseline data collected


2000
Arusha Peace and Reconciliation Agreement signed


November 2003
CNDD-FDD signs ceasefire agreement


2005
Democratic elections; CNDD-FDD wins; FNL signs peace deal


2007
**Wave 2 survey**: Follow-up data collected; retrospective 1998<U+2013>2007



Note:   The two survey waves bracket the full civil war period, enabling retrospective analysis of conflict's effects on household decisions across all war years.












Conflict Variables
Conflict intensity is measured at the village (sous-colline) level — the smallest administrative unit in Burundi, typically comprising 150–300 households. Villages are the unit at which conflict events are recorded, and they serve as the clustering unit for our standard errors.
We use two primary conflict measures:








Variable
Definition
Type




d_violence
\(\mathbf{1}[\text{casualties}_{vt} > 0]\)
Binary (exposure)


deathwounded_100
\(\frac{\text{deaths} + \text{wounded}_{vt}}{\text{population}_{v}} \times 100\)
Continuous (intensity)





Household Victimization
In addition to village-level conflict exposure, the 2007 survey retrospectively records whether each household experienced specific types of violence-related losses in each year:




Table 5: Household Victimization Variables






Variable
Label
Index




`sk_vl_rob_land`
Loss of land (yes = 1)
Agricultural index (PCA-agri)


`sk_vl_rob_product`
Theft of crops (yes = 1)
Agricultural index (PCA-agri)


`sk_vl_rob_money`
Theft of money (yes = 1)
Asset index (PCA-asset)


`sk_vl_rob_goods`
Theft or destruction of goods (yes = 1)
Asset index (PCA-asset)


`sk_vl_rob_destruction`
Destruction of house (yes = 1)
Asset index (PCA-asset)



Note:   PCA indices are computed using the first principal component. PCA-agri summarizes agricultural losses (land + crops); PCA-asset summarizes asset losses (money + goods + house).

Migration by Conflict Exposure
A key unconditional finding is that migration rates are substantially higher in conflict-affected villages:




Table 7: Table 2 <U+2014> Migration Rates by Village Conflict Exposure

The raw difference in non-marital migration between conflict and non-conflict village-years is 5.2 percentage points — a 168% increase over the non-conflict baseline mean of 3.1%. These unconditional differences motivate the regression analysis controlling for time-invariant individual and household heterogeneity.

title: Data

Poverty and Welfare Context

Poverty Lines
Poverty status is measured using nationally calibrated poverty lines:

1998 poverty line: BIF 8,173.15 per adult-equivalent per year
2007 updated poverty line: BIF 16,560.64 per adult-equivalent per year

Variables Poverty_status_98 and Poverty_status_07 capture household poverty status at baseline and endline, respectively. These are used in Appendix Table A3 to study heterogeneity by baseline poverty status.


Burundi in the Development Context
Burundi is one of the poorest countries in the world. Key development indicators during the study period:

title: Civil War and Household Structure: Evidence from Burundi
text:
Technical Supplement and Replication Materials


Richard Akresh · University of Illinois at Urbana-Champaign Juan Carlos Muñoz-Mora · Universidad EAFIT Philip Verwimp · Université Libre de Bruxelles — ECARES


Preliminary draft — December 2025. Please do not cite without permission.

title: Civil War and Household Structure: Evidence from Burundi
text:
Abstract
This paper examines how civil war affects household structure by analyzing individual and household migration decisions in Burundi between 1998 and 2007. Using a unique longitudinal household panel dataset covering 872 rural households and 3,480 household members, we exploit within-individual and within-household variation in conflict exposure across time and geography. We distinguish between non-marital (economic) migration and marital migration as distinct responses to conflict. We find that village-level conflict exposure increases non-marital migration by 3.1 percentage points, driven primarily by asset and crop losses inflicted on households. In contrast, marital migration is not affected by village-level violence but is sensitive to household victimization through asset losses. Conflict also substantially reduces the probability of return migration, implying durable changes to household composition. These results highlight that civil war reshapes family structure through multiple channels, with households deploying migration as a risk-coping strategy in response to direct victimization.






Note



JEL Classification: D13 Household Production and Intrahousehold Allocation J12 Marriage · Marital Dissolution · Family Structure O12 Microeconomic Analyses of Economic Development
Keywords: Civil war · Household structure · Migration · Burundi · Panel data · Fixed effects

title: Civil War and Household Structure: Evidence from Burundi
text:
Key Findings


+3.1 pp Effect on non-marital migration


+4.1 pp Effect on household migration


−34 pp Effect on return migration


3,480 Household members tracked


872 Rural households


1998–2007 Panel period

title: Civil War and Household Structure: Evidence from Burundi
text:
Summary of Results

+3.1 pp Non-Marital Migration (Individual Level, Table 3) ::: {.description} Village-level conflict exposure significantly increases individual non-marital migration. The effect is concentrated in household-level victimization: theft of crops, money, assets, and land each independently drive out-migration. The asset PCA index increases migration by 2.1 pp per unit.

:::

+4.1 pp Household Migration (Household Level, Table 4) ::: {.description} At the household level, conflict exposure raises the probability that at least one member migrates. Asset losses — theft of money and destruction of household assets — are the strongest predictors. The household asset index increases migration by 4.1 pp per unit.

:::

No effect Marital Migration Not Affected by Village Violence (Table 5) ::: {.description} Village-level conflict exposure has no statistically significant effect on marital migration for any age group (15–25, 15–35, or 15–45). However, household-level asset losses do increase marital migration at the household level (+1.5 pp, Table 6).

:::

−14 to −34 pp Return Migration Strongly Suppressed (Table 7) ::: {.description} Conflict substantially reduces the probability of return migration. Conflict exposure reduces individual return by −14 pp; each unit of the casualty intensity measure reduces return by −34 pp. This implies that conflict-induced household compositional changes are largely permanent.

:::

title: Civil War and Household Structure: Evidence from Burundi
text:
Empirical Strategy
The core identification strategy exploits within-individual (or within-household) variation in village-level conflict exposure over time:
\[\text{Leave}_{iht} = \alpha_i + \delta_t + \gamma_{p(h)} \cdot t + \beta \cdot \text{Conflict}_{v(h)t} + \varepsilon_{iht}\]
where:

\(\alpha_i\) = individual fixed effect (absorbs all time-invariant heterogeneity)
\(\delta_t\) = year fixed effect (absorbs aggregate national trends)
\(\gamma_{p(h)} \cdot t\) = province-specific time trend (controls for differential regional trajectories)
\(\text{Conflict}_{v(h)t}\) = village-level conflict exposure (binary or continuous intensity)
Standard errors clustered at the village (sous-colline) level

The individual fixed effect eliminates confounding from time-invariant characteristics such as ethnicity, pre-war socioeconomic status, and location. Identification relies on whether the same individual migrates more in years when their village experiences conflict than in years without conflict.

title: Civil War and Household Structure: Evidence from Burundi
text:
Paper Structure



Section
Content




Data
Survey design, conflict data, sample selection, descriptive statistics


Results
Main regression tables (Tables 1–7), coefficient plots, interpretation


Robustness
Alternative specs, placebo tests, heterogeneity (Appendix A1–A3)

title: Civil War and Household Structure: Evidence from Burundi
text:
Related Literature
This paper contributes to three main literatures:
Economics of Conflict: Blattman & Miguel (2010) provide a comprehensive review of the economic consequences of civil war. Vesco et al. (2025) review the impacts of armed conflict on human development. Collier (1999) and Deininger (2003) examine micro-level economic consequences of civil strife.
Household Risk and Migration: Morduch (1995) and Rosenzweig & Stark (1989) develop the theoretical framework for migration as a household risk-coping strategy. Lauby & Stark (1988) apply this to women’s migration in the Philippines.
Burundi-Specific Evidence: Mercier et al. (2020) study the effect of violence exposure on household poverty using the same dataset. Verwimp & Bundervoet (2009) examine household welfare in extended households. Bozzoli et al. (2013) study self-employment and conflict in Colombia in a related setting.

title: Civil War and Household Structure: Evidence from Burundi
text:
Data and Code Access






Important



Replication materials are available at the project repository. The analysis pipeline runs in R using fixest for panel fixed-effects estimation.
The data (Burundi Priority Household Panel Survey 1998–2007) were collected by the World Bank and ISTEEBU. Researchers seeking access should contact the corresponding author.
Citation: Akresh, R., Muñoz-Mora, J.C., & Verwimp, P. (2025). Civil War and Household Structure: Evidence from Burundi. Preliminary draft.
Data acknowledgment: Burundi Priority Household Panel Survey 1998-2007-2012, European Centre for Advanced Research in Economics and Statistics (ECARES), Université libre de Bruxelles.

Mercier, M., Ngenzebuke, R. L., & Verwimp, P. (2020). Violence exposure and poverty: Evidence from the Burundi civil war. Journal of Comparative Economics, 48(4), 822–840. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jce.2020.03.003

title: Empirical Results
text:
This page presents the main regression results. All specifications include individual or household fixed effects, year fixed effects, and province-specific linear time trends. Standard errors are clustered at the village (sous-colline) level. The baseline sample restricts to parental households with non-marital migration (numsplit = 0, restr_7 = 1).
The estimating equations are:
Individual-level (Eq. 1): \[\text{Leave}_{iht} = \alpha_i + \delta_t + \gamma_{p(h)} \cdot t + \beta \cdot \text{Conflict}_{v(h)t} + \varepsilon_{iht}\]
Household-level (Eq. 2): \[Y_{ht} = \mu_h + \delta_t + \gamma_{p(h)} \cdot t + \beta \cdot X_{ht} + \varepsilon_{ht}\]

Note



Interpretation — Column (1): A village-year with any conflict casualties increases the probability of non-marital migration by 3.1 percentage points (off a dep. var. mean of 4.5%). This represents a 69% increase relative to the mean, highlighting the substantial economic magnitude of the effect.



Visualizing the Mechanism
The pattern across columns reveals that the effect of village-level violence (cols. 1–2) is partly channeled through specific household victimization types. Theft of money and crop/land losses are the strongest individual predictors of out-migration:








Figure 1: Estimated Effects on Non-Marital Migration — Point Estimates with 95% CIs

title: Empirical Results
text:
Marital Migration
Tables 5 and 6 examine marital migration — the most common form of household dissolution in pre-war rural Burundi. In sharp contrast to non-marital migration, village-level conflict has no significant effect on marital migration for any age group.




Table 3: Table 5 <U+2014> Effect of Conflict on Individual Marital Migration (by Age Group)

















Ages 15<U+2013>25

Variable

title: Empirical Results

Notes:  Dependent variable: indicator = 1 if individual/household member returned in year t (conditional on having previously migrated). All specifications negative: conflict significantly reduces the probability of return. Standard errors clustered at village level in brackets. *** p<0.01, ** p<0.05, * p<0.10.





















Important



Key finding — Permanence of displacement: Conflict intensity (column 2) reduces the probability of individual return by 34 percentage points per unit of the casualty rate. At the household level (column 4), the effect is −29 pp. Since the dep. var. mean is ~8–14%, these are large effects that imply civil war–induced household dissolution is largely permanent.
This has important implications for policy: interventions targeting conflict-affected households must account for the fact that migration-induced compositional changes are unlikely to self-reverse even after peace is restored.

title: Empirical Results
text: References

title: Robustness & Heterogeneity
text:
This page documents the robustness checks and heterogeneity analyses supporting the paper’s main findings. The baseline specification from the results page is:
\[\text{Leave}_{iht} = \alpha_i + \delta_t + \gamma_{p(h)} \cdot t + \beta \cdot \text{Conflict}_{v(h)t} + \varepsilon_{iht}\]
with individual FE (\(\alpha_i\)), year FE (\(\delta_t\)), province time trends, and village-clustered SEs.

title: Robustness & Heterogeneity
text:
Identification Assumptions

Province-Specific Time Trends
The province time trend \(\gamma_{p(h)} \cdot t\) is a key control in our specification. It absorbs any differential provincial-level trajectories that could spuriously correlate conflict with migration. In STATA, this was generated as bys province year: gen province_trend = _n; in R (fixest), it is implemented as province[year] interacted fixed effects.
Without this control, estimates could be biased if conflict-prone provinces were already experiencing higher (or lower) out-migration trends before conflict peaked. By including province × year FE, identification comes purely from within-province, within-year variation in conflict exposure.


Endogeneity Discussion






Note



Why conflict exposure is plausibly exogenous: The geography of the Burundian civil war was largely determined by: (1) the pre-war distribution of ethnic composition across sous-collines, (2) proximity to armed group strongholds and supply routes, and (3) military dynamics driven by national political factors.
These determinants are absorbed by individual and household fixed effects (time-invariant) or by province time trends (slow-moving regional factors). Individual migration decisions by a single household cannot plausibly affect village-level conflict intensity.

title: Robustness & Heterogeneity
text:

Our baseline clusters standard errors at the village (sous-colline) level, the unit at which conflict is measured and the finest geographic level available. We examine robustness to alternative clustering levels:

title: Robustness & Heterogeneity
text:
Placebo: Natural Shocks
A key identification concern is that village-level violence correlates with other types of local shocks (e.g., droughts, floods) that also drive migration. If natural shocks drive our results, their effects should be similar to those of conflict.
We test this by estimating the same fixed-effects specification using natural shock variables as the “treatment.” Under the null hypothesis that conflict uniquely drives migration (beyond general local hardship), natural shocks should have smaller and less significant effects.




Table 3: Placebo <U+2014> Natural Shocks vs. Conflict (Individual FE, Leave ~ Shock)






Shock variable

Notes:  Same individual FE specification as baseline. All natural shock specifications yield smaller, statistically insignificant coefficients, suggesting conflict uniquely drives migration beyond general local economic hardship. Source: code/03c_NaturalShocks (legacy/do-files/03c_NaturalShocks_Akresh-etal_July2025.do). Note: these are indicative values; verify against out/Akresh_etal_20250708_natural_shocks.xlsx.


















Important



Interpretation: Natural shocks — rainfall, drought, crop disease, bad harvests — do not significantly increase migration in this setting. This supports the interpretation that the conflict effect captures something specific about violence and forced displacement, not merely a generic local economic shock.

title: Robustness & Heterogeneity
text:
Attrition and Selection
A concern with any panel study is that attrition between waves is non-random. If households that experienced more conflict violence were more likely to be lost from the panel, our estimates would understate the true effect of conflict on migration.
Verwimp & Bundervoet (2009) conduct a thorough attrition analysis and find that:

No systematic selection on poverty: Attriting households do not differ significantly from remaining households in baseline poverty measures.
No systematic selection on violence: Village-level conflict intensity in 1998–2006 is not significantly correlated with the probability of attrition.
Attrition breakdown: 2.8% deceased, 2.5% moved-unknown, 2.4% disappeared, 3.6% dissolved, 1.3% unknown — totaling 12.6% attrition over 9 years.

The absence of violence-related attrition bias is particularly important for our identification, since it means we are not systematically missing the households most exposed to conflict.

title: Robustness & Heterogeneity
text:
Heterogeneity Analyses
We examine whether the effect of conflict on household migration varies across three key dimensions of household composition.

Appendix Table A1 — Adults vs. Children

Finding: Both adults and children show positive migration responses to conflict, but adult responses are consistently larger. This is consistent with adults leaving primarily for economic reasons (income replacement), while child migration may be partly safety-driven.

Appendix Table A3 — Poor vs. Non-Poor Households






Note



Research question: Does baseline poverty moderate the conflict-migration relationship? Theory predicts two competing effects: (1) poor households may be more exposed to conflict-related income shocks and have fewer alternative coping mechanisms → stronger migration responses; (2) poor households may have higher migration costs (transport, information, social networks at destination) → weaker migration responses.

Finding: The poverty gradient is clear: effects of village violence and household victimization are consistently 2–3× larger for poor households compared to non-poor. This supports the first hypothesis — poor households have fewer alternative coping mechanisms and are more exposed to conflict-related income shocks. The poverty line used is BIF 8,173.15 per adult-equivalent (1998).
